text: The aim of this video is to demonstrate our preferred technique for laparoscopic extended right amicolectomy with the primary vascular approach and radical lymphadenectomy. An eighty-one-year-old lady was investigated for anemia and weight loss, and an adenocarcinoma of the hepatic flexure was diagnosed. Preoperative imaging did not demonstrate evidence of nodal involvement or metastatic disease.

The aim of this video is to demonstrate our preferred technique for laparoscopic extended right amicolectomy with the primary vascular approach and radical lymphadenectomy. An eighty-one-year-old lady was investigated for anemia and weight loss, and an adenocarcinoma of the hepatic flexure was diagnosed. Preoperative imaging did not demonstrate evidence of nodal involvement or metastatic disease.

After establishing pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon inserts two 5mm ports suprapubically and in the epigastric region, and a 12mm operating trocar in the left iliac fossa. Using a medial to lateral no-touch technique, the ileocecal junction is retracted laterally to expose the ileocolic vessels. The mesenteric window is opened below the ileocolic vessels, and the right retrocolic space is developed cranially and laterally along the avascular plane of Toldt. A retrocecal appendix is visible. The surgeon approaches the ileocolic pedicle, using a Thunderbeat vessel sealing device to dissect and ligate the ileocolic vein and artery at the origin of the superior mesenteric vessels.

After establishing pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon inserts two 5mm ports suprapubically and in the epigastric region, along with a 12mm trocar in the left iliac fossa. Using a medial to lateral no-touch technique, the ileocecal junction is retracted laterally to expose the ileocolic vessels. The mesenteric window is opened below the ileocolic vessels, and the duodenum is identified as the right retrocolic space is developed cranially and laterally along the avascular plane of Tholt. A retrocecal appendix is visible. The surgeon approaches the ileocolic pedicle, using a Thunderbit vessel sealing device to dissect and ligate the ileocolic vein and artery at the origin of the superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to progressively separate the duodenum from the transverse colon, ensuring the duodenum remains visible in the operative field to prevent thermal injury. The right colic artery is not identified during this dissection.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to progressively separate the duodenum from the transverse colon, ensuring the duodenum remains visible in the operative field to prevent thermal injury.
The surgeon dissects and separately ligates the right branches of the middle colic vessels at their origin, located on the superior aspect of the pancreas.
The surgeon dissects the right branches of the middle colic vessels and ligates them separately at their origin, located on the superior surface of the pancreas.

The surgeon completes the procedure by fully mobilizing the hepatic flexure from below, adhering to the no-touch technique principles. After interrupting the vascular supply, the right colon is mobilized laterally. The specimen is delivered through a five-centimeter right-sided transverse incision, followed by the creation of an unsewn end-to-side aliotransverse anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the procedure by fully mobilizing the hepatic flexure from below, adhering to the no-touch technique principles. After interrupting the vascular supply, the right colon is mobilized laterally. The specimen is delivered through a five-centimeter right-sided transverse incision, followed by the creation of an unsewn end-to-side aliotransverse anastomosis.

The post-operative recovery was uneventful and the patient was discharged home on post-operative day four. Histopathology demonstrated a T-free cancer with none out of twenty-two lymph nodes involved.
